title: Ringing or buzzing in the ears
excerpt: A persistent ringing or buzzing in the ears, known in Ayurveda as karna-nada, is understood as an imbalance of vata in the head and ears. Classical texts offer gentle, time-tested remedies that work alongside modern care.
body_html:
that sound no one else can hear

You are sitting quietly, perhaps reading or trying to sleep, and there it is: a high-pitched ring, a low hum, or a faint buzzing that comes from nowhere and goes nowhere. There is no fan on, no traffic outside. The sound is entirely inside your head or your ears. For many people in India this is a familiar and mildly maddening experience, something they have mentioned to a family member or quietly dismissed as fatigue. Modern medicine calls it tinnitus. Ayurveda gave it a name thousands of years ago: karna-nada, literally "sound in the ear."

Karna means ear, and nada means sound or resonance. The condition appears across several classical texts as one of the diseases of the ear canal and the sense organ of hearing. The Sushruta Samhita, in its section on ear diseases (Shrotragata Roga), lists karna-nada among the conditions that arise from disturbance in this region. Madhava Nidana, the classical diagnostic text, similarly includes it in the nidana (causation and presentation) of ear disorders. Knowing the name and the classical lineage matters because it connects your lived experience to a coherent explanatory framework rather than leaving it as a vague annoyance.

the vata explanation

In Ayurvedic thinking, the five senses are each governed by one of the five elements. The ear, and the sense of hearing, is governed by akasha (space) and vayu (air). These two together form what is called the vata dosha, the force that governs all movement and transmission in the body, including the transmission of sound vibrations along the auditory nerve. When vata becomes aggravated and accumulates in the head and ears, it disrupts the delicate channels (srotas) that carry prana to the sense organs.

The Ashtanga Hridaya, one of the most studied of the classical compendia, describes vata's role in disorders of the head and sense organs (Uttarasthana). Aggravated vata in the ear channels is understood to generate the abnormal internal sounds that characterise karna-nada. What aggravates vata? The classical causes are consistent: excessive dry or cold food, irregular sleep, chronic worry, prolonged exposure to loud noise, suppression of natural urges, and the natural increase of vata that comes with age and with autumn and early winter seasons (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

This does not mean every case of tinnitus is purely a vata condition. Depending on a person's constitution and history, kapha or even pitta can be involved, producing variations in the character of the sound. But vata aggravation is the most common pattern described in the classical literature for this kind of symptom.

everyday habits that help

Ayurvedic management of karna-nada focuses on pacifying vata through warmth, oil, regularity, and quiet. These are not dramatic interventions; they are small daily adjustments that, when kept consistently, create the conditions for the nervous system to settle.

Karna-purana, or ear oiling, is the most specific classical recommendation. A few drops of warm sesame oil or medicated oil like bilva taila are placed in the ear canal while lying on one side, held for a few minutes, and then drained. The Sushruta Samhita and Ashtanga Hridaya both describe karna-purana as a routine practice for maintaining the health of the ear and as a therapeutic step when ear complaints arise. Start gently, once or twice a week, ideally in the morning before a bath. Use only plain sesame oil unless a vaidya prescribes a specific medicated preparation.

Abhyanga, the daily self-massage with warm sesame oil, is described throughout the classical texts as a powerful vata-pacifying practice (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Pay attention to the scalp, the back of the neck, and the area around the ears. This is not merely cosmetic; oil and warmth on the skin signal safety to the nervous system and help reverse the dryness and constriction that feed vata disorders.

Diet and rhythm matter enormously. Warm, cooked, mildly spiced food eaten at regular times is the Ayurvedic default for anyone with vata aggravation. Cold, raw, very dry, or very pungent food tends to worsen the condition. Adequate sleep at a consistent hour is not optional; vata increases with sleep deprivation and irregular rest. Reducing exposure to very loud environments, wearing ear protection where noise is unavoidable, and carving out even ten minutes of silence in the day all work in the same direction.

Nasya, the practice of placing a few drops of warm sesame or plain ghee in each nostril in the morning, is mentioned in classical texts as a route to nourish and clear the channels of the head (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). It is gentle and can be done at home. Avoid it if you have a cold or active sinus congestion.

when to see a doctor

The habits described above are supportive measures for mild, occasional ringing that you recognise as stress-related or season-related. They are not a substitute for medical evaluation. Please consult a doctor or qualified vaidya if the sound is new and sudden, if it is loud and persistent rather than intermittent, if you have hearing loss alongside it, if only one ear is affected, if you feel dizziness or a spinning sensation (vertigo), or if the sound pulses in time with your heartbeat. These can indicate conditions such as fluid in the ear, middle-ear infection, acoustic neuroma, high blood pressure, or other causes that need proper diagnosis and treatment. This article is general information only. It is not a diagnosis and does not replace professional medical or Ayurvedic care.

Try This Today

  • Warm ear oiling (karna-purana): Lie on your side. Warm a teaspoon of plain sesame oil until it feels comfortable on your wrist. Place 3 to 5 drops in the upper ear using a clean dropper. Stay still for 5 minutes, then drain onto a tissue. Do this once or twice a week, not during an active ear infection.
  • Morning scalp and neck oil massage: Before your bath, massage warm sesame oil into your scalp, the back of your neck, and the area around your ears for 5 to 10 minutes. Leave it on for at least 15 minutes before washing off. This is a classic vata-pacifying routine from the Ashtanga Hridaya.
  • Nasya (nasal drops): Tilt your head back slightly. Place 2 drops of warm plain ghee or sesame oil in each nostril in the morning, ideally 20 minutes before breakfast. Sniff gently. Skip this on days when your nose feels congested.
  • Eat warm, cooked, and regular: Have your meals at fixed times each day. Prioritise warm, lightly spiced cooked food: dal, khichdi, sabzi with a little ghee. Reduce cold drinks, raw salads, and very dry or crunchy food, especially in the evening.
  • Create a daily silence window: Set aside 10 to 15 minutes each day in a quiet spot, no phone, no background TV. Sit comfortably and breathe slowly. This is not meditation by prescription; it is simply reducing the vata-aggravating stimulus load that modern life piles onto the auditory system.

metafields.dek: A persistent ringing or buzzing in the ears, known in Ayurveda as karna-nada, is understood as an imbalance of vata in the head and ears. Classical texts offer gentle, time-tested remedies that work alongside modern care.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Karna-nada (ear ringing) is linked to aggravated vata disturbing the subtle channels of hearing.
Warm ear oiling (karna-purana) with sesame oil is the primary classical remedy described in the Sushruta Samhita.
Regular sleep, warm cooked food, and reducing loud noise form the everyday vata-pacifying foundation.

glossary_terms.definition: Literally 'sound in the ear'; the Ayurvedic name for tinnitus or ear ringing.
The practice of filling the ear canal with warm medicated or plain oil as a preventive and therapeutic measure.
The dosha governing all movement and transmission in the body; composed of space and air elements; linked to the sense of hearing.
The subtle channels through which prana and sensory impulses travel; disruption in these underlies many Ayurvedic disease explanations.
Nasal administration of drops of oil or ghee to nourish and clear the channels of the head.
Daily warm-oil self-massage, described in classical texts as a foundational vata-pacifying practice.

sources: Sushruta Samhita, Uttarasthana, Karna Roga (ear disease) chapters
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, daily regimen and sense-organ care; Uttarasthana, diseases of the head and ears
Madhava Nidana, Chapter on ear disorders (Karna Roga Nidana)
Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana, causes and management of vata aggravation

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Karna-nada" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Karna-nada is defined as: Literally 'sound in the ear'; the Ayurvedic name for tinnitus or ear ringing.

This is a classic vata-pacifying routine from the Ashtanga Hridaya.
